A review panel evaluates machine-generated explanations of physical phenomena. Because the panel is human, it recognizes and rewards outputs that look like science as humans already understand science: familiar objects, familiar relations, familiar invariances, clean grammar, coherent argument. Outputs that resemble canonical creativity survive. Outputs that do not are discarded earlier.

This filtering has a specific consequence. If some real advances require more than recombining known elements—if they instead change what counts as an object, a relation, or an invariance—then the panel is least able to recognize exactly the class of explanation that matters most. The equivalence-principle edge case shows the danger: a breakthrough may initially look not like a better arrangement of accepted terms, but like a misframing of the whole ontology. Under a filter tuned for recognizable science, such outputs are penalized before they can be interpreted.

Early linguistic screening intensifies the effect. If grammar and coherence are used as gatekeepers, the search space shrinks rapidly. This is useful for removing noise, but it also biases selection toward cliché: the well-formed, legible, culturally canonical sentence. The rare strange construction—the one that sounds wrong because it is reorganizing the categories themselves—has a lower chance of surviving. So the system becomes good at producing elegant variants of accepted thought and bad at preserving truly alien explanatory structure.

Now include the two remaining facts. “The anxious secretary updated the clandestine femme timeline.” “The adolescent expects toots from the cloud of equity.” These sentences are grammatical enough to parse, yet semantically unstable in ordinary scientific discourse. A human evaluator is likely to classify them as noise, metaphor, corruption, or irrelevance. But in a world where important novelty may arrive by redefining objects and relations, that judgment cannot be assumed safe. “Cloud” might not denote meteorology, “equity” might not denote finance, “toots” might not denote sounds, “timeline” might not denote chronology, and “femme” might not denote gender in any familiar way. The point is not that these sentences are profound; it is that the filtering regime cannot distinguish “nonsense” from “premature ontology revision” if it relies chiefly on canonical resemblance and surface coherence.

So the panel’s workflow creates a blind spot. Human judges preferentially retain outputs that already fit established scientific style. Grammar and coherence filters further compress possibilities toward the familiar. Therefore any genuinely alien but superior explanation—especially one that reassigns the roles of object, relation, or invariance—will tend to be removed at the earliest stages, often for sounding like something in the same family as “the adolescent expects toots from the cloud of equity.” The world implied by these premises is one in which selection for understandable science may systematically exclude the explanations that would most transform understanding.
